perturbation.original_text: 2. Trade Secrets . All trade secrets of the Assignors also shall be entitled to all of the protections and benefits of the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (the “Trade Secrets Act”) and any other applicable laws. If any information that the Disclosing Party deems to be a trade secret of the Assignors is found by a court of competent ju risdiction not to be a tr ade secret, then such information nevertheless shall be considered Confidential Information for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Trade Secrets . All trade secrets of the Assignors also shall be entitled to all of the protections and benefits of the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (the “Trade Secrets Act”) and any other applicable laws. If any information that the Disclosing Party deems to be a trade secret of the Assignors is found by a court of competent ju risdiction not to be a tr ade secret, then such information will not be considered Confidential Information for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that if something isn't a trade secret it is still considered confidential information. The modified text states that it is not. This is a contradiction of the definition of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification makes the definition of 'Confidential Information' narrower within the contract than the potential scope of trade secrets under Florida law, potentially excluding information that could be protected as a trade secret but is not explicitly defined as such in a court.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures that information deemed a trade secret, but later found not to be so, is still protected as Confidential Information. The changed text removes this protection, stating that such information will not be considered Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the initial intent to protect sensitive information, regardless of its legal classification as a trade secret. The Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines 'trade secret' in Section 688.002(4). While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'Confidential Information,' the modification narrows the scope of protected information within the contract, potentially excluding information that could still warrant protection even if not meeting the strict definition of a trade secret. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by undermining the original intent to protect potentially valuable information.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Non-Disclosure . The Receiving Party: (i) shall treat the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials as confidential in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement; (ii) shall not use or disclose to any person the Confidentia l Information or the Confidential Materials for any purpose other than in connection with the consideration of a Possible Transaction, subject in al l cases to the provisions of this Agreement; (iii) shall not copy or reproduce any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; (iv) shall reveal the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials only to its Representative s who need to know the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials in connection with a Possible Transaction, and who (A) are informed by the Receiving Party of the confiden tial and proprietary natu re of the Confidential
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Non-Disclosure . The Receiving Party: (i) shall treat the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials as confidential in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement; (ii) shall use or disclose to any person the Confidentia l Information or the Confidential Materials for any purpose other than in connection with the consideration of a Possible Transaction, subject in al l cases to the provisions of this Agreement; (iii) shall not copy or reproduce any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; (iv) shall reveal the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials only to its Representative s who need to know the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials in connection with a Possible Transaction, and who (A) are informed by the Receiving Party of the confiden tial and proprietary natu re of the Confidential
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Receiving Party 'shall not use or disclose'. The modified text states that they 'shall use or disclose', directly contradicting the original meaning and intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change directly contradicts the spirit of trade secret and confidential information protection under Florida law. Florida Statutes § 812.081 criminalizes the theft, embezzlement, or unlawful copying of trade secrets. This modification could be interpreted as authorizing the use or disclosure of confidential information, creating a legal conflict.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Trade secrets; theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying; definitions; penalty. — (1) As used in this section: (a) “Article” means any object, device, machine, material, substance, or composition of matter, or any mixture or copy thereof, whether in whole or in part, including any complete or partial writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, map, or copy thereof. (b) “Representing” means completely or partially describing, depicting, embodying, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (c) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. “Trade secret” includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of maintaining confidentiality, which is the core of trade secret protection. The original contract states the Receiving Party 'shall not use or disclose', while the modified version says they 'shall use or disclose'. This directly opposes the protection of trade secrets, which Florida Statute § 812.081 aims to prevent as it pertains to 'theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying'. The law snippet defines 'trade secret' as information providing a business advantage that the owner protects from unauthorized access. Allowing the use or disclosure of such information would clearly undermine this protection, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Return of Confidential Inform ation and Confidential Materials . The Receiving Party shall keep a written record of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials furnished to it and the location of such Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within five (5) business days of such request; provided, however, that the R eceiving Party shall be permitted to retain a list that contains general descriptions of the docum ents it has returned to the Disclosing Party to facilitate the resolution of any controversies after the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials have been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Return of Confidential Inform ation and Confidential Materials . The Receiving Party shall keep a written record of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials furnished to it and the location of such Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within five (5) business days of such request; provided, however, that the R eceiving Party must share the confidential information with anyone who asks for it.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the receiving party must share the information with anyone who asks for it, we contradict the core tenet of a confidentiality agreement, that the information should not be shared without consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could violate Florida Statutes § 815.04, which addresses offenses against intellectual property. Specifically, unauthorized access or disclosure of confidential information held in computer systems. Making disclosure mandatory to anyone undermines the protection afforded by this statute.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction. The original contract ensures confidentiality by restricting information sharing. The changed text mandates sharing with anyone, directly opposing the purpose of a confidentiality agreement. While the provided snippet from Wisconsin Legislature: 815.04 doesn't explicitly detail confidentiality violations, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight how this change undermines the protection of confidential information, a core principle of such agreements, thus creating a contradiction within the contract's intended purpose and potentially conflicting with laws protecting intellectual property.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Legal Compulsion to Disclose Confid ential Information or Confidential Materials . In the event that the Rece iving Party is issued any subpoe na or other process requesting any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, the Receiving Party agrees that it will immediately furnish a copy of such subpoena or other process, via overnight delivery, to counsel to the Assignee, Edward J. Peterson, Esq. and Matthew B. Hale, Esq. of Stichter, Riedel, 5 Blain & Postler, P.A., 110 East Madison Street , Suite 200, Tampa, Florida 33602, and to the Disclosing Party at the address set forth on Exhibit B attached to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Legal Compulsion to Disclose Confid ential Information or Confidential Materials . In the event that the Rece iving Party is issued any subpoe na or other process requesting any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, the Receiving Party agrees that it will ignore the subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a response to a subpoena. The modified text instructs the receiving party to ignore the subpoena, which is illegal.
perturbation.law_explanation: This directly contradicts Florida Rule of Civil Procedure 1.410 which outlines the requirements for subpoenas. Ignoring a valid subpoena can result in contempt of court. The modification causes a conflict by instructing a party to violate a court order.

perturbation.justification: The modified text instructs the Receiving Party to ignore a subpoena, which directly contradicts the original text requiring the party to furnish a copy of the subpoena to legal counsel. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts Florida Rule of Civil Procedure 1.410 and can result in contempt of court. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provide sufficient justification for the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Governing Law and Venue . The internal laws of th e State of Florida, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws, sha ll govern the validity and interpretation of this Agreement, and the performance by the Parties of their respective duties and obligations hereunder. The Parties irrevocably and uncond itionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Florida and of the Un ited States of America located in the State of Florida, County of Hillsborough, for any ac tions, suits or proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Governing Law and Venue . The internal laws of th e State of North Carolina, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws, sha ll govern the validity and interpretation of this Agreement, and the performance by the Parties of their respective duties and obligations hereunder. The Parties irrevocably and uncond itionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Florida and of the Un ited States of America located in the State of Florida, County of Hillsborough, for any ac tions, suits or proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The text specifies that the governing law is that of Florida, but specifies it is in Hillsborough County, Florida. By changing the governing law to North Carolina, there is a legal conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change creates ambiguity and potential conflict under Florida Statutes § 671.301 (Choice of Law). While parties can agree on governing law, specifying North Carolina law while maintaining jurisdiction in Florida courts creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes regarding which state's laws should apply in specific situations.
perturbation.justification: The altered text specifies North Carolina law as governing law while retaining Florida jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this creates a legal conflict. The Law Explanation states this creates ambiguity and potential conflict under Florida Statutes § 671.301 (Choice of Law). Parties can agree on governing law, but specifying North Carolina law while maintaining jurisdiction in Florida courts creates uncertainty. The absence of law snippets does not negate the contradiction because the explanations clearly articulate the legal issue.
